license.notice: Event descriptions are paraphrased summaries written for this dataset. Source links point to authoritative references.

entries.title: AppLocker allowed executable (audit mode)
entries.summary: An executable or DLL was allowed to run, but would have been blocked if AppLocker were in enforcement mode.
entries.details: Generated when AppLocker is in audit mode and an executable (EXE) or DLL would have been blocked by the active rule set but was allowed to run. Key fields: FileName (the full path of the allowed file), UserName (the account that ran it), PolicyName, and RuleId. This event is the primary source for baselining and policy tuning — it reveals what would break if enforcement were enabled. In incident response, EID 8003 entries on a system where AppLocker is in audit mode confirm that unsigned or unapproved binaries executed successfully. FileName paths in user-writable directories (AppData, Temp, Downloads, Desktop) are high priority. Correlate with Security log EID 4688 or Sysmon EID 1 for process creation context.

prerequisites.description: AppLocker must be configured via Group Policy and set to audit mode for this event to be generated.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools

investigationPivots: FileName in user-writable directories (AppData, Temp, Downloads) is high priority — legitimate software rarely runs from these paths
Correlate UserName with expected users — service accounts running executables interactively is anomalous
Use PolicyName and RuleId to understand which AppLocker rule the file violated — 'No rules match' means the file fell outside all defined allow rules
commonFalsePositives: All software not yet added to the AppLocker allow policy during initial policy development
Portable applications that run from non-standard directories by design

entries.title: AppLocker blocked executable (enforcement mode)
entries.summary: An executable or DLL was blocked from running by AppLocker policy.
entries.details: Generated when AppLocker actively blocks an EXE or DLL from executing in enforcement mode. Key fields: FileName, UserName, PolicyName, and RuleId. This is the highest-value AppLocker event in enforcement environments — it directly records blocked execution attempts. Adversaries who reach a well-configured endpoint and attempt to run tools, droppers, or lateral movement payloads will generate this event. FileName in unexpected paths (Temp, AppData, Downloads, or shares) combined with a privileged UserName is a critical indicator. Note that DLL enforcement is often disabled by default even when EXE enforcement is active, as it has a higher performance impact. Correlate with Security log EID 4625 or 4688 for additional context.

prerequisites.description: AppLocker must be configured via Group Policy and set to enforcement mode for this event to be generated.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools

investigationPivots: FileName is the primary artifact — hash it and submit to threat intel / VirusTotal
Repeated blocks against different filenames from the same UserName may indicate an adversary probing the policy
Blocks of known attacker tools (mimikatz, cobalt strike stagers, common RATs) should trigger immediate incident response
FileName in network paths (\\server\share\...) indicates attempted remote execution
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate software deployed to machines before being added to the AppLocker allow policy
IT tools run by administrators from non-standard paths

entries.title: AppLocker allowed script or MSI (audit mode)
entries.summary: A script or MSI installer was allowed to run but would have been blocked in enforcement mode.
entries.details: Generated when AppLocker is in audit mode and a script (PowerShell, VBScript, JScript, batch file) or MSI installer would have been blocked by the active rule set but was permitted to run. Key fields: FileName, UserName, PolicyName, and RuleId. Script-based execution is one of the most common attacker vectors — this channel captures PowerShell scripts, WScript/CScript execution, and MSI-based installers that are not covered by EXE allow rules. Particular interest should be paid to FileName values in user-writable directories or network paths, and to scripts with randomized or obfuscated filenames. Correlate with PowerShell log EID 4104 (script block logging) for the actual script content.

prerequisites.description: AppLocker must be configured via Group Policy and set to audit mode for this event to be generated.

investigationPivots: Combine FileName with PowerShell log EID 4104 to retrieve the actual script content
Scripts in TEMP, AppData, or network shares are higher priority than scripts in Program Files
MSI files outside of standard software distribution paths (SCCM, WSUS, vendor shares) are suspicious
commonFalsePositives: Administrative scripts run by IT staff from non-standard locations
Software installers (MSI) from vendors not yet added to the allow policy

entries.title: AppLocker blocked script or MSI (enforcement mode)
entries.summary: A script or MSI installer was blocked from running by AppLocker policy.
entries.details: Generated when AppLocker actively blocks a script or MSI from executing in enforcement mode. Key fields: FileName, UserName, PolicyName, and RuleId. Script execution blocks are high-value detection signals because adversaries frequently use scripts (PowerShell, VBScript, JScript, batch files) as initial execution vehicles. An attacker who gains code execution via a document macro or web download and then attempts to run a PowerShell payload will generate this event before potentially pivoting to an AppLocker bypass technique (T1218 — signed binary proxy execution via mshta.exe, regsvr32.exe, etc.). Correlate with subsequent execution events to detect bypass attempts.

prerequisites.description: AppLocker must be configured via Group Policy and set to enforcement mode for this event to be generated.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools

investigationPivots: Look for follow-on execution events using LOLBAS tools that may indicate an AppLocker bypass attempt — LOLBins: mshta.exe, regsvr32.exe, rundll32.exe; LOLBAS Scripts: wscript.exe with /b flag, cscript.exe; LOLBAS Libraries: .dll files invoked via rundll32.exe or regsvr32.exe /s
FileName with a .ps1 extension being blocked, followed by PowerShell execution via encodedcommand, is a common evasion pivot
Correlate UserName with logon events to understand attacker access level at time of block
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate scripts deployed by IT before being whitelisted
Software installers triggered by users outside of managed deployment channels

entries.title: AppLocker allowed packaged app (audit mode)
entries.summary: A packaged (UWP/MSIX) app was allowed to run but would have been blocked in enforcement mode.
entries.details: Generated when AppLocker is in audit mode and a Universal Windows Platform (UWP) or MSIX packaged application would have been blocked by the active rule set but was permitted to run. Key fields: FileName (the package name or path), UserName, and RuleId. Packaged app rules are often overlooked in AppLocker policy configurations. Malicious MSIX packages have been used as delivery vehicles in phishing campaigns (MSIX AppInstaller abuse, CVE-2021-43890). This event captures packaged app execution that falls outside the allow policy.

prerequisites.description: AppLocker must be configured via Group Policy and set to audit mode for this event to be generated.

investigationPivots: FileName (package name) should be compared against known legitimate UWP apps from the Microsoft Store
Sideloaded packages from unsigned or unknown publishers are high priority
Correlate with EID 8022/8023 to see if the package was also installed (not just executed) recently
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate Store apps not yet added to the AppLocker packaged app allow policy
Enterprise LOB packaged apps deployed via MDM

entries.title: AppLocker blocked packaged app (enforcement mode)
entries.summary: A packaged (UWP/MSIX) app was blocked from running by AppLocker policy.
entries.details: Generated when AppLocker actively blocks a UWP or MSIX packaged application from executing in enforcement mode. Key fields: FileName (package name or path), UserName, and RuleId. Malicious MSIX packages have been used as a phishing delivery mechanism and as a bypass for certain application whitelisting controls. A packaged app block on a known-malicious package name or an unsigned sideloaded package is a high-confidence indicator. This event should be investigated alongside the package installation log (EID 8023) to determine how the package arrived on the system.

prerequisites.description: AppLocker must be configured via Group Policy and set to enforcement mode for this event to be generated.

investigationPivots: Identify how the package was installed — check recent file creation in AppX staging directories and browser download history
Look for AppInstaller URI handler activity if the block follows a web redirect
Verify the publisher certificate on the blocked package — self-signed or unknown publishers are suspicious
commonFalsePositives: Enterprise LOB packaged apps deployed before being added to the allow policy
Developer machines with sideloaded apps under development

entries.title: AppLocker audited packaged app installation (audit mode)
entries.summary: A packaged (UWP/MSIX) app installation was allowed but would have been blocked in enforcement mode.
entries.details: Generated when AppLocker is in audit mode and a UWP or MSIX packaged application installation would have been blocked by the active rule set but was permitted to proceed. Distinct from EID 8020 (execution audited) — EID 8022 fires at installation time, while EID 8020 fires when the already-installed app runs. Malicious MSIX packages have been widely used as phishing delivery vectors (MSIX AppInstaller abuse, CVE-2021-43890). Correlate with EID 8020 to track when an audited-but-installed package is subsequently launched.

prerequisites.description: AppLocker must be configured via Group Policy and set to audit mode for packaged app deployment rules. The Microsoft-Windows-AppLocker/Packaged app-Deployment log channel must be enabled.

investigationPivots: FileName (package name or path) — check the package publisher certificate; self-signed or unknown publishers are suspicious
Correlate with browser download history and EID 4688 (AppInstaller.exe process creation) to determine how the package arrived on the system
Cross-reference with EID 8020 to confirm whether the installed package was subsequently executed
Package installation from AppInstaller URI handler (via a browser redirect) is a common phishing delivery pattern
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate Store apps sideloaded for enterprise deployment not yet added to the AppLocker allow policy
Developer machines installing test packages before they are submitted to the allow policy

entries.title: AppLocker blocked packaged app installation (enforcement mode)
entries.summary: A packaged (UWP/MSIX) app installation was blocked by AppLocker policy.
entries.details: Generated when AppLocker actively blocks the installation of a UWP or MSIX packaged application in enforcement mode. The earliest detection point in the MSIX phishing kill chain — fires at installation time before execution is possible. The combination of EID 8023 (install blocked) and EID 8021 (execution blocked) confirms both installation and execution were prevented.

prerequisites.description: AppLocker must be configured via Group Policy and set to enforcement mode for packaged app deployment rules. The Microsoft-Windows-AppLocker/Packaged app-Deployment log channel must be enabled.

investigationPivots: FileName identifies the blocked package — submit the package name to threat intelligence; known malicious packages have well-documented IOCs
Investigate how the user attempted to install the package — browser history, ms-appinstaller URI handler invocations, and email attachments are the common delivery vectors
Correlate with EID 4688 for any AppInstaller.exe or winget.exe process creation preceding the block
A block followed immediately by an EID 8021 (execution blocked) for the same package indicates the payload may have been partially staged but was denied execution
commonFalsePositives: Enterprise LOB apps deployed before being added to the AppLocker allow policy
IT teams installing packages for testing or evaluation purposes

detectionRules.title: Packaged App Installation Blocked by AppLocker
detectionRules.rule:
Event
| where Source == "Microsoft-Windows-AppLocker"
| where EventID == 8023
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, UserName, RenderedDescription
| order by TimeGenerated desc
detectionRules.notes: Combine with EID 8021 (execution block) for the same package to confirm full kill chain prevention. Investigate delivery method via browser history and AppInstaller.exe process creation events.

detectionRules.title: AppLocker Blocked Packaged App Installation
detectionRules.rule:
title: AppLocker Blocked Packaged App Installation
status: experimental
logsource:
    product: windows
    service: applocker
detection:
    selection:
        EventID: 8023
    condition: selection
falsepositives:
    - Enterprise apps not yet in the AppLocker allow policy
    - IT testing and evaluation of packages
level: medium
detectionRules.notes: Escalate to high if the package name matches known malicious MSIX payload names or is delivered via an ms-appinstaller URI handler from an external URL.
